text.title: bash-aliases Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for bash-aliases as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Aliasing a command to itself with different flags, creating an infinite recursion loop.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: alias rm="rm -rf" calls rm which calls rm again infinitely until the process limit is hit, making rm unusable. Use a different name or a function with \rm to bypass the alias.

failure-mode.description: Missing quotes around alias values containing spaces or special characters.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: alias ll=ls -la (unquoted) only aliases ll to ls; the -la is parsed as a separate token and ignored. The alias silently misbehaves rather than throwing an error.

failure-mode.description: Putting complex logic (if/else, loops, subshells) directly in alias values.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Bash aliases do not support compound commands. They appear to work in simple cases but break in subtle ways with quoting, piping, and argument expansion. Use functions for any logic.

failure-mode.description: Forgetting to source ~/.bash_aliases after making changes and wondering why new aliases do not work.
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Aliases are loaded at shell startup. Editing the file does not update the running shell. Use `source ~/.bash_aliases` or a reload alias defined early in the file.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Mixing aliases into .bashrc makes the file harder to manage, harder to sync via dotfiles, and prevents isolated reload with a single source command.
